claim: The strong positional constraints on word-initial glyphs {o,c,q,s,d} and word-final glyphs {y,n,l,r,o} are artifacts of a structured nulls-and-affixes system rather than natural phonotactics: removing these positional glyphs as null markers should produce a residual corpus whose glyph entropy approaches Latin's ~4.0 bits
evidence_for: Glyph entropy 3.8627 bits is measurably below Latin (~4.0) and Italian (~3.95), consistent with entropy suppression from null/affix glyphs
Positional constraints are among the highest-confidence beliefs in the system (0.624, 0.608, 0.605)
Zipf exponent 0.8946 is sub-Zipfian, which can result from systematic morphological prefixing/suffixing that splits natural-language word types
evidence_against: Natural Semitic or agglutinative languages also exhibit strong positional constraints without nulls
If residual entropy after stripping still falls below 3.9 bits, a pure nulls explanation is insufficient
test: Remove all word-initial tokens from {o,c,q,s,d} and all word-final tokens from {y,n,l,r,o} at the glyph level; recompute glyph-level Shannon entropy and Zipf exponent on the residual glyph sequence
test_metric: Residual glyph entropy (bits) and Zipf exponent R-squared after stripping positional glyphs
test_threshold: Confirmation if residual glyph entropy rises to â‰¥ 3.95 bits (within 0.05 of Italian); denial if residual entropy remains â‰¤ 3.87 bits or R-squared drops below 0.85

tests_run.test: remove all word-initial tokens from {o,c,q,s,d} and all word-final tokens from {y,n,l,r,o} at the gl
